1CH.author: Unknown, conventionally called "the Chronicler." Traditionally identified with Ezra. Linguistic and theological connections with Ezra-Nehemiah are noted but debated — some scholars see one author, others see shared tradition.
1CH.date: c. 400–300 BCE, among the latest books in the Hebrew Bible. Uses Samuel-Kings as a source (verbal parallels are extensive and verifiable).
1CH.genre: Genealogy (chapters 1–9) and theological history (chapters 10–29). Retells material found in Samuel with consistent, identifiable editorial changes.
1CH.summary: 1 Chronicles retells Israel's history from Adam through David, with the genealogies establishing community identity and the David narrative focused almost entirely on temple worship and its organization.

1KI.author: Unknown. Originally one book with 2 Kings. Part of the Deuteronomistic History. The text references its own sources by name: "the Book of the Acts of Solomon" (11:41), "the Book of the Annals of the Kings of Israel" (14:19), and "the Book of the Annals of the Kings of Judah" (14:29). These source texts are lost.
1KI.date: Final compilation during or after the Babylonian exile (6th century BCE), using sources from the 10th–7th centuries BCE.
1KI.genre: Royal chronicle, prophetic narrative, and temple construction account. Each king receives a formulaic evaluation.
1KI.summary: 1 Kings narrates Solomon's reign and the construction of the temple, then the kingdom's fracture into north (Israel) and south (Judah), with the prophet Elijah confronting royal idolatry.

1SA.author: Unknown. Originally one book with 2 Samuel (divided for scroll length in the Septuagint tradition). Part of the Deuteronomistic History. The text draws on multiple sources — prophetic narratives, court records, and at least two different accounts of how Saul became king (compare chapters 9–10 with chapter 8 and 10:17–27).
1SA.date: Sources may date to the 10th–9th century BCE. Final compilation 7th–6th century BCE.
1SA.genre: Political narrative, prophetic biography, and court history. Contains poetry (Hannah's song, 2:1–10; David's lament, anticipated in the narrative).
1SA.summary: 1 Samuel narrates the transition from tribal confederation to monarchy through three figures — the prophet Samuel, the first king Saul, and his rival David — presenting the institution of kingship as both desired by the people and deeply ambivalent.

2CH.author: The Chronicler (same as 1 Chronicles).
2CH.genre: Theological history focused on the Southern Kingdom (Judah), its kings, and the temple.
2CH.summary: 2 Chronicles tells the story of Judah's kings from Solomon to the exile, evaluated by their faithfulness to temple worship, and ends with Cyrus's decree permitting the rebuilding — the final words of the Hebrew Bible in its traditional ordering.

2KI.author: Unknown. The conclusion of the Deuteronomistic History. References the same lost annals as 1 Kings. The final verses (25:27–30) record the release of King Jehoiachin from Babylonian prison in 561 BCE, providing a terminus post quem for the final edition.
2KI.date: Final form after 561 BCE. Sources span the 9th–6th centuries BCE.
2KI.genre: Royal chronicle and prophetic narrative. The formulaic king evaluations continue throughout.
2KI.summary: 2 Kings narrates the decline and destruction of both Israelite kingdoms — the north falling to Assyria in 722 BCE, the south to Babylon in 586 BCE — with the Deuteronomistic editors explaining both as consequences of covenant violation.

2SA.author: Unknown. Originally one book with 1 Samuel. The "Court History" or "Succession Narrative" (chapters 9–20, continuing into 1 Kings 1–2) has been called one of the earliest examples of historical prose in world literature, though this assessment is debated.
2SA.date: The Succession Narrative may date to the 10th–9th century BCE (close to the events). Final compilation 7th–6th century BCE.
2SA.genre: Court history, political narrative, poetry (David's lament in chapter 1, David's last words in chapter 23, the psalm in chapter 22), and prophetic oracle.
2SA.summary: 2 Samuel narrates David's reign — his consolidation of power, the establishment of Jerusalem as capital, the dynastic promise, and then his personal failures and the catastrophic family and political consequences that follow.

AMO.author: Amos, a sheep breeder (noqed) from Tekoa in Judah (1:1), who also tended sycamore-fig trees (7:14). He explicitly denies being a professional prophet: "I was no prophet, nor a prophet's son" (7:14) — though the Hebrew syntax (lo navi anokhi) could also be translated "I am not a prophet."
AMO.date: Mid-8th century BCE, during the reigns of Uzziah of Judah and Jeroboam II of Israel (1:1). The opening reference to an earthquake "two years before the earthquake" anchors the chronology — a major earthquake c. 760 BCE is attested archaeologically at Hazor (stratum VI destruction layer) and referenced centuries later in Zechariah 14:5.
AMO.genre: Prophetic oracle — judgment oracles, woe oracles, vision reports, and a biographical narrative (7:10–17).
AMO.summary: Amos is the earliest writing prophet whose words survive as a separate book — a Judean outsider delivering an unwelcome message of divine judgment to a prosperous Northern Kingdom, grounding that judgment in social injustice and economic exploitation.

DAN.author: The text presents Daniel as a Judean exile in Babylon from the first deportation (605 BCE per 1:1). Chapters 1–6 narrate events about Daniel in third person; chapters 7–12 contain first-person visions. The book is written in two languages: Hebrew (1:1–2:4a, 8:1–12:13) and Aramaic (2:4b–7:28).
DAN.date: The historical setting is the Babylonian and Persian courts (6th–5th century BCE). The visions in chapters 7–12 describe events matching the Hellenistic period with remarkable precision — particularly the career of Antiochus IV Epiphanes (175–164 BCE). The detailed accuracy about Antiochus and the abrupt loss of accuracy after 164 BCE (11:40–45 does not match Antiochus's actual death) is the primary evidence for dating the final form to c. 167–164 BCE, during the Maccabean crisis.
DAN.genre: The book combines two genres: court tales (chapters 1–6, comparable to the Joseph narrative and the book of Esther) and apocalyptic visions (chapters 7–12 — the first fully developed apocalyptic literature in the Hebrew Bible).
DAN.summary: Daniel combines Babylonian court tales with apocalyptic visions — written in two languages, set in the exile but addressing the Hellenistic crisis — introducing concepts (resurrection, angels by name, the "son of man" figure, calculated end-times) that profoundly shaped both Jewish and Christian thought.

DEU.author: Presented as Moses' speeches. The book itself does not claim Mosaic authorship of the whole — it records Moses speaking, with a third-person narrator framing the speeches and narrating Moses' death (chapter 34). Scholars widely associate the core of Deuteronomy with the "Book of the Law" discovered in the Jerusalem temple during Josiah's reign in 622 BCE (2 Kings 22:8). The relationship between that discovered document and the present book of Deuteronomy is debated. The text underwent editing during and after the Babylonian exile.
DEU.date: Core material possibly 8th–7th century BCE. Final form 6th–5th century BCE. The name "Deuteronomy" comes from the Greek deuteronomion ("second law"), a mistranslation of the Hebrew mishneh hattorah (17:18), which means "a copy of this law."
DEU.genre: Speeches (three major addresses by Moses), law code, covenant renewal text, poetry, and blessing. The structure closely mirrors ancient Near Eastern treaty forms.
DEU.summary: Deuteronomy presents Moses' final speeches to Israel — restating the law, renewing the covenant, and framing the choice between faithfulness and disaster — before his death on the border of the promised land.

ECC.author: The text identifies the speaker as "Qoheleth, son of David, king in Jerusalem" (1:1) — implying Solomon. However, the Hebrew contains Persian loanwords (pardes, "park/garden," 2:5; pitgam, "sentence/decree," 8:11), Aramaic grammatical forms, and late Hebrew features that place the language squarely in the post-exilic period. The Solomonic frame is a literary device — the author writes in the voice of a king who has tried everything.
ECC.date: c. 300–250 BCE, based on linguistic analysis. Among the latest books in the Hebrew Bible.
ECC.genre: Wisdom literature of a philosophical, exploratory kind. Part first-person reflection, part observation, part instruction. Shares features with Greek philosophical traditions (Epicureanism, Stoicism, Skepticism) but is rooted in the Israelite wisdom tradition.
ECC.summary: Ecclesiastes is a sustained meditation on the word hebel — "vapor, breath, absurdity" — observing that human life, achievement, wisdom, and pleasure are all fleeting and beyond ultimate human control.

EST.author: Unknown. The text demonstrates detailed knowledge of Persian court protocol, the layout of Susa, and Persian administrative terminology.
EST.date: The story is set during the reign of Xerxes I (Ahasuerus, 486–465 BCE). Composition likely 4th–3rd century BCE, based on linguistic evidence and the use of Persian loanwords.
EST.genre: Court tale, political thriller, and festival etiology (origin story for Purim). Rich in literary devices: irony, reversal, banquet structure, and dark comedy.
EST.summary: Esther narrates how a Jewish woman who became queen of Persia, together with her cousin Mordecai, averted a state-sponsored attempt to annihilate all Jews in the empire — the story behind the festival of Purim.

EXO.author: The text does not name its author. Traditional attribution to Moses. Scholarly analysis identifies multiple literary sources (J, E, P) interwoven by editors, with the final form likely dating to the exilic or post-exilic period. The question of authorship remains debated.
EXO.date: The events described are set in the Late Bronze Age (c. 15th–13th century BCE). The text reached its final written form during or after the Babylonian exile (6th–5th century BCE). The Song of the Sea (15:1–18) contains archaic Hebrew grammar and may be among the oldest passages in the Bible, possibly dating to the 12th–11th century BCE based on linguistic analysis.
EXO.genre: Narrative (chapters 1–18), law code (chapters 19–24), and architectural/liturgical instruction (chapters 25–40). Three distinct genres bound together.
EXO.summary: Exodus narrates the departure of the Israelites from Egypt, the covenant at Sinai, and the construction of a portable sanctuary — establishing the legal and ritual framework that defines Israel for the rest of the Bible.

EZK.author: The text identifies the author as "Ezekiel son of Buzi, the priest" (1:3), exiled to Babylon in the first deportation of 597 BCE. Unlike Isaiah and Jeremiah, there is minimal scholarly debate about Ezekiel's authorship of the core material, though editorial expansions are identified.
EZK.date: 593–571 BCE, based on 14 precise dates given in the text (the most precisely dated prophetic book). The first vision is dated to "the fifth year of King Jehoiachin's exile" (1:2) = 593 BCE. The last dated oracle is from the 27th year (29:17) = 571 BCE.
EZK.genre: Prophetic vision, symbolic action, allegory, oracle against nations, and detailed architectural/ritual prescription (chapters 40–48).
EZK.summary: Ezekiel is the work of a priest-prophet in Babylonian exile who describes extraordinary visions — the divine chariot-throne, the valley of dry bones, a future temple — while explaining the fall of Jerusalem as the departure of divine glory from a defiled sanctuary.

EZR.author: Unknown. Traditionally attributed to Ezra. Originally one book with Nehemiah. Connections to the Chronicler's work are debated.
EZR.date: Events span 538–458 BCE. Composition likely 4th century BCE.
EZR.genre: Historical narrative with embedded official documents (royal decrees and letters). Bilingual — contains significant Aramaic sections (4:8–6:18, 7:12–26) alongside Hebrew.
EZR.summary: Ezra narrates the return of Jewish exiles from Babylon, the rebuilding of the Jerusalem temple, and Ezra the scribe's arrival to reform the community — culminating in the controversial dissolution of marriages to non-Jewish women.

GEN.author: The text does not name its author. The Torah (Genesis–Deuteronomy) is traditionally attributed to Moses in Jewish, Christian, and Islamic traditions. Since the 18th century, scholars have identified multiple literary sources within Genesis — commonly labeled J (Yahwist), E (Elohist), D (Deuteronomist), and P (Priestly) — woven together by later editors. The Documentary Hypothesis remains influential but is debated and has been modified in many forms. What is broadly agreed: Genesis is a composite text that reached its final form over a long period.
GEN.date: The oral traditions behind Genesis are ancient, with some material plausibly reaching into the 2nd millennium BCE. The written compilation most likely reached its final form during or after the Babylonian exile (6th–5th century BCE). The earliest physical manuscript evidence comes from Dead Sea Scrolls fragments (c. 250 BCE–68 CE). The oldest complete Hebrew text is the Leningrad Codex (c. 1008 CE).
GEN.genre: A blend of genres: cosmogony (origin story), genealogy, ancestral narrative, etiological tales (stories explaining origins of names, places, customs), covenant texts, and poetry. Chapters 1–11 differ markedly in style and scope from chapters 12–50.
GEN.summary: Genesis narrates the origins of the world, humanity, and one particular family line — from Abraham through Isaac, Jacob, and Joseph — whose story sets up everything that follows in the Bible.

HAB.author: Habakkuk the prophet (1:1, 3:1). Nothing else is known. The name's etymology is uncertain — possibly from an Akkadian plant name (hambaququ).
HAB.date: The reference to the Chaldeans/Babylonians (kasdim, 1:6) as a rising power places the book c. 610–600 BCE — after the fall of Nineveh (612 BCE) and before the fall of Jerusalem (586 BCE), during the period of Babylonian expansion under Nebuchadnezzar.
HAB.genre: Prophetic dialogue (unique — the prophet questions the deity and receives answers), woe oracles, and a theophanic hymn (chapter 3) with musical notations.
HAB.summary: Habakkuk is a prophetic dialogue in which the prophet questions divine justice — why does the deity use a more wicked nation to punish Judah? — and receives an answer that "the righteous shall live by his faithfulness."

HAG.author: Haggai the prophet (1:1). Nothing else is known about him.
HAG.date: The most precisely dated book in the Hebrew Bible. Four oracles are dated to specific days in 520 BCE: August 29 (1:1), October 17 (2:1), December 18 (2:10), and December 18 (2:20) — all in the second year of Darius I of Persia.
HAG.genre: Prophetic oracle — short, direct speeches calling for action.
HAG.summary: Haggai is four precisely dated oracles from 520 BCE urging the returned exiles to rebuild the temple — the most concretely situated prophetic book in the Bible.

HOS.author: Hosea son of Beeri (1:1), a prophet of the Northern Kingdom (Israel/Ephraim).
HOS.date: Mid-8th century BCE. The superscription names kings Uzziah through Hezekiah of Judah and Jeroboam II of Israel (1:1), placing Hosea's ministry c. 750–720 BCE — the last decades before the Northern Kingdom's fall to Assyria (722 BCE).
HOS.genre: Prophetic oracle, biographical narrative (chapters 1–3), covenant lawsuit, and some of the most difficult Hebrew poetry in the Bible.
HOS.summary: Hosea uses his marriage to an unfaithful wife as the central metaphor for Israel's relationship with its deity — a text of fierce accusation and fierce love, written in Hebrew so difficult that many verses remain genuinely uncertain.

ISA.author: The text attributes the book to "Isaiah son of Amoz" (1:1). Since the late 18th century, scholars have identified at least two, possibly three, distinct voices: First Isaiah (chapters 1–39, primarily 8th century BCE), Second Isaiah / Deutero-Isaiah (chapters 40–55, 6th century exilic period), and Third Isaiah / Trito-Isaiah (chapters 56–66, post-exilic period). The evidence includes differences in historical setting (Assyrian crisis vs. Babylonian exile vs. return), vocabulary, style, and theological emphasis. The unity of authorship is maintained by some scholars. The book as a whole functions as a literary unit under Isaiah's name.
ISA.date: First Isaiah: c. 740–700 BCE. Second Isaiah: c. 550–540 BCE. Third Isaiah: c. 530–500 BCE. Final compilation 5th century BCE or later.
ISA.genre: Prophetic oracle, poetry, vision, covenant lawsuit (riv), woe oracle, narrative (chapters 36–39), hymn, and proto-apocalyptic (chapters 24–27, the "Isaiah Apocalypse").
ISA.summary: Isaiah moves from judgment on a faithless nation under Assyrian threat to comfort for exiles in Babylon to a vision of cosmic renewal — the largest and most literarily complex prophetic book in the Bible.

JDG.author: Unknown. Part of the Deuteronomistic History. The text was compiled from older tribal traditions, hero stories, and possibly written records, shaped by editors working in the 7th–6th century BCE.
JDG.date: The stories describe the period c. 1200–1020 BCE (Iron Age I). Final compilation 7th–6th century BCE.
JDG.genre: Cyclical narrative, hero tales, tribal conflict accounts, poetry (the Song of Deborah), and etiological stories. The literary quality ranges from archaic poetry to sophisticated narrative art.
JDG.summary: Judges narrates a repeating cycle of crisis and deliverance through military leaders ("judges"), with each cycle worse than the last, culminating in civil war and near-tribal extinction.

JER.author: The text attributes the book to the prophet Jeremiah son of Hilkiah, from the priestly town of Anathoth in Benjamin (1:1). A scribe named Baruch son of Neriah is identified as writing Jeremiah's dictation (36:4, 32). A bulla (clay seal impression) reading "Belonging to Berekhyahu son of Neriyahu the scribe" was published in 1975 — possibly Baruch himself, though the provenance is unprovenanced (antiquities market), limiting its evidential value.
JER.date: Jeremiah's ministry spans c. 627–586 BCE (1:2–3). The book reached its final form over an extended period — the Septuagint and Masoretic versions differ so substantially that they represent two distinct editorial traditions.
JER.genre: Prophetic oracle, prose sermon, biographical narrative, lament, symbolic action report, letter (chapter 29), and covenant lawsuit.
JER.summary: Jeremiah documents the final decades of the Kingdom of Judah, warning of Babylonian destruction, witnessing the fall of Jerusalem, and articulating the concept of a "new covenant" — all preserved in two significantly different textual traditions.

JOB.author: Unknown. The text does not identify its author. Job is not presented as an Israelite — he is from "the land of Uz" (1:1), likely located in Edom or northern Arabia based on geographical references (Teman, Shuhite, Naamathite — all associated with the Transjordan/Arabian region).
JOB.date: Debated. The prose frame (chapters 1–2, 42:7–17) may be older; the poetic dialogues contain some of the most difficult Hebrew in the Bible with archaic forms and possible Aramaic influence. Proposals range from the 7th to the 4th century BCE. No consensus exists.
JOB.genre: Wisdom literature. A prose frame story (chapters 1–2, 42:7–17) wraps an extended poetic dialogue (chapters 3–42:6). Also contains hymnic poetry (chapter 28), legal disputation, nature poetry (chapters 38–41), and lament.
JOB.summary: Job is a poetic exploration of undeserved suffering — a righteous man loses everything, his friends insist suffering implies guilt, he demands an answer from the deity, and the answer he receives is not the one he expected.

JOL.author: Joel son of Pethuel (1:1). Nothing else is known about him.
JOL.date: The most debated dating question among the Minor Prophets. Proposals range from the 9th century to the 4th century BCE. The absence of any mention of a king, the references to priests and elders as community leaders (1:13–14, 2:16–17), the mention of "Greeks" (Yevanim, 3:6/4:6 in Hebrew), and the post-exilic liturgical vocabulary suggest a late date — most likely 5th–4th century BCE.
JOL.genre: Prophetic oracle with liturgical elements (communal lament, call to fasting, divine response).
JOL.summary: Joel moves from a devastating locust plague to a cosmic vision of the "Day of YHWH" — introducing language and imagery that became foundational for both Jewish and Christian eschatology.

JON.author: Anonymous. The book is a narrative about Jonah son of Amittai, not written by him. The Jonah son of Amittai in 2 Kings 14:25 is a prophet from Gath-hepher in the Northern Kingdom during Jeroboam II's reign (8th century BCE).
JON.date: The Hebrew contains late linguistic features (Aramaisms, late vocabulary) suggesting post-exilic composition, most likely 5th–4th century BCE. The book presupposes Nineveh's destruction (612 BCE) as past — 3:3 says Nineveh "was" (hayetah) a great city, using a past tense.
JON.genre: Prophetic narrative — but unique among the prophets. It is the only prophetic book that is almost entirely narrative rather than oracular. Its genre is debated: historical account, parable, satire, or didactic novella.
JON.summary: Jonah is the story of a prophet who runs from his commission, is swallowed by a great fish, reluctantly delivers a five-word oracle to Nineveh, and is angry when the city repents — a narrative that subverts prophetic conventions and raises questions about the scope of divine mercy.

JOS.author: The text does not name its author. Traditionally attributed to Joshua. It is part of the Deuteronomistic History (Joshua–2 Kings), compiled in the 7th–6th century BCE with later editing. The text includes the phrase "to this day" multiple times, indicating temporal distance from the events described.
JOS.date: Sources may include ancient traditions (boundary lists, city lists, the Shechem covenant). Final form likely 6th century BCE, with possible post-exilic editing.
JOS.genre: Military narrative, land allotment records, boundary descriptions, and covenant ceremony. Chapters 1–12 are dramatic narrative; chapters 13–21 are administrative geography.
JOS.summary: Joshua narrates the Israelite entry into Canaan, the conquest of key cities, and the division of the land among the twelve tribes — presenting the fulfillment of the territorial promise made to Abraham.

LAM.author: The text is anonymous. The attribution to Jeremiah comes from the Septuagint's introduction ("And it came to pass, after Israel was taken captive... Jeremiah sat weeping and lamented this lamentation over Jerusalem") and from the Talmud (Baba Bathra 15a), not from the Hebrew text itself. The linguistic and theological differences from the book of Jeremiah have led most scholars to conclude the author is unknown.
LAM.date: Shortly after 586 BCE — the destruction of Jerusalem by Nebuchadnezzar. The poems describe the destruction as recent and raw.
LAM.genre: Lament poetry — specifically city laments. The ancient Near Eastern genre of the city lament is well attested: the Sumerian "Lamentation over the Destruction of Ur" (c. 2000 BCE) and the "Lamentation over the Destruction of Sumer and Ur" share the same genre — poems mourning the destruction of a city and its temple by divine decision.
LAM.summary: Lamentations is five poems of grief over the destruction of Jerusalem and its temple in 586 BCE, composed as acrostic poetry in a genre with Mesopotamian antecedents stretching back two millennia.

LEV.author: The text does not name its author. Traditional attribution to Moses. Scholarly consensus associates the bulk of the material with the Priestly source (P), with a distinct section called the Holiness Code (chapters 17–26, designated H) that may represent an independent collection incorporated into the larger work. The relationship between P and H is actively debated.
LEV.date: The ritual practices described may be ancient, but the written compilation is generally dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th–5th century BCE). Dead Sea Scrolls include Leviticus fragments, with 11QpaleoLev written in paleo-Hebrew script.
LEV.genre: Ritual law, purity regulations, and ethical instruction. Almost entirely prescriptive — instructions for what to do, when, and how. Narrative is minimal (the Nadab/Abihu episode in chapter 10 and the blasphemer case in 24:10–23 are the exceptions).
LEV.summary: Leviticus is a manual of ritual and ethical instruction that defines categories of sacrifice, purity, impurity, priesthood, and holiness — all organized around the premise that the deity described in the text dwells physically among the people and this proximity requires careful regulation.

MAL.author: "Malachi" may be a proper name or a title — mal'akhi means "my messenger" and may derive from 3:1 ("I will send my messenger"). The Septuagint translates it as "his messenger" rather than treating it as a name. The Targum attributes the book to Ezra. The author is anonymous if "Malachi" is a title.
MAL.date: Post-exilic, after the Second Temple's completion (516 BCE). The issues addressed — priestly negligence, intermarriage, tithing failure — overlap with those in Ezra-Nehemiah, suggesting c. 475–450 BCE.
MAL.genre: Prophetic oracle using a distinctive disputational style — six dialogue units where the deity makes a statement, the audience challenges it ("How have you loved us?"), and the deity responds.
MAL.summary: Malachi is the last book in the Christian Old Testament — a series of disputations between the deity and a disillusioned post-exilic community, closing with the promise of Elijah's return before "the great and dreadful day of YHWH."

MIC.author: Micah of Moresheth (1:1), a town in the Shephelah (Judean foothills), southwest of Jerusalem. He is a rural prophet, not from the capital.
MIC.date: The superscription places him during the reigns of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah (1:1), c. 750–700 BCE — contemporary with Isaiah in Jerusalem and Hosea in the north.
MIC.genre: Prophetic oracle — judgment, covenant lawsuit (riv, chapter 6), and restoration oracles.
MIC.summary: Micah delivers judgment on both Samaria and Jerusalem for social injustice and corrupt leadership — a rural prophet whose words include both "Bethlehem Ephrathah" and "what does YHWH require of you?"

NAH.author: Nahum the Elkoshite (1:1). The location of Elkosh is unknown — proposals include a site in Galilee, southern Judah, and even Mesopotamia (Al-Qosh in northern Iraq, near Nineveh).
NAH.date: Between the fall of Thebes (No-Amon, 663 BCE, referenced as past in 3:8) and the fall of Nineveh (612 BCE, which the book anticipates). Most likely c. 650–612 BCE.
NAH.genre: Prophetic oracle against a foreign nation — specifically an extended poem celebrating Nineveh's impending destruction. The superscription calls it both a massa ("oracle/burden") and a sefer chazon ("book of the vision").
NAH.summary: Nahum is a sustained poetic celebration of Nineveh's coming destruction — some of the most vivid and violent war poetry in the Hebrew Bible, composed by a prophet from a nation that had suffered under Assyrian brutality.

NEH.author: Partly a first-person memoir ("I, Nehemiah" — the "Nehemiah Memoir," chapters 1–7, 12:27–43, 13), edited into a larger work. Originally one book with Ezra.
NEH.date: Events c. 445–430 BCE. Composition 4th century BCE.
NEH.genre: Memoir, narrative, administrative lists, and covenant text. The first-person sections are distinctive in biblical literature.
NEH.summary: Nehemiah narrates how a Jewish official in the Persian court returned to Jerusalem, rebuilt its walls in 52 days against opposition, and led communal reforms — including a public Torah reading, covenant renewal, and enforcement of Sabbath and marriage regulations.

NUM.author: The text does not name its author. Traditional attribution to Moses. Scholarly analysis identifies a blend of Priestly (P) material and older narrative sources (J, E). The final compilation dates to the exilic or post-exilic period.
NUM.date: The traditions span from potentially ancient oral material to a final compilation in the 6th–5th century BCE.
NUM.genre: Census data, travel itinerary, law, narrative, poetry, and prophetic oracle. The most genre-diverse book in the Torah.
NUM.summary: Numbers narrates the wilderness period between Sinai and the border of Canaan — a period dominated by census-taking, rebellion, divine response, and the transition from one generation to the next.

OBA.author: Obadiah (1:1). Nothing else is known. The name is common in the Hebrew Bible (over a dozen individuals).
OBA.date: The reference to Jerusalem's destruction and Edom's complicity (vv. 10–14) most likely places the book after 586 BCE. Some scholars argue for a pre-exilic date based on the Jeremiah 49 parallels.
OBA.genre: Prophetic oracle against a foreign nation — the shortest book in the Hebrew Bible (21 verses).
OBA.summary: Obadiah is a single chapter condemning Edom for its complicity in Jerusalem's destruction — the shortest book in the Hebrew Bible, preserving a bitter memory of betrayal by a neighboring kinsman-people.

PRO.author: A collection of collections. The text identifies multiple sources: "Proverbs of Solomon son of David" (1:1, 10:1), "Proverbs of Solomon copied by the men of Hezekiah king of Judah" (25:1), "Words of the Wise" (22:17), "Sayings of Agur son of Jakeh" (30:1), and "Sayings of King Lemuel — an oracle his mother taught him" (31:1). Agur and Lemuel are otherwise unknown; Lemuel may be non-Israelite.
PRO.date: Individual proverbs may date to the monarchic period (10th–7th century BCE). The collection was assembled over centuries, reaching final form by the post-exilic period (5th–3rd century BCE). The editorial note in 25:1 ("copied by the men of Hezekiah") documents a specific stage in the compilation process.
PRO.genre: Wisdom literature. Chapters 1–9 contain extended wisdom poems; chapters 10–31 contain mostly short, two-line sayings (the mashal — a compressed observation).
PRO.summary: Proverbs is a curated collection of practical observations about how life works — covering speech, work, relationships, money, character, and the pursuit of wisdom — grounded in the premise that there is an observable moral order in the world.

PSA.author: Multiple authors. Superscriptions attribute 73 psalms to David, 12 to Asaph, 11 to the Sons of Korah, 2 to Solomon, 1 to Moses (Psalm 90), 1 to Heman, 1 to Ethan, and 49 are anonymous. Whether le-David ("of David") means "by David," "for David," "dedicated to David," or "in the Davidic style" is grammatically ambiguous in Hebrew.
PSA.date: Individual psalms span centuries — from possibly the pre-monarchic period (Psalm 29, which shows close parallels to Ugaritic poetry) to the post-exilic period (Psalm 137, "By the rivers of Babylon"). The collection was compiled gradually, reaching its final 150-psalm form by the 2nd century BCE.
PSA.genre: Poetry — specifically liturgical poetry. Scholars classify psalms by type: hymns of praise, individual laments, communal laments, thanksgiving psalms, royal psalms, wisdom psalms, pilgrimage psalms (120–134, the "Songs of Ascent"), and imprecatory psalms.
PSA.summary: Psalms is a collection of 150 poems that were sung, chanted, and prayed in Israelite worship — expressing the full range of human experience from ecstatic praise to bitter despair.

RUT.author: Unknown. The literary polish, the genealogical ending connecting Ruth to David, and the narrator's explanatory note about sandal customs (4:7 — "now this was the custom in former times in Israel") all indicate a writer at some distance from the events described.
RUT.date: The story is set "in the days when the judges ruled" (1:1). Composition proposals range from the early monarchy (10th century BCE, close to the events) to the post-exilic period (5th–4th century BCE). The language contains both archaic features and late elements, making dating difficult.
RUT.genre: Short story or novella — four chapters of carefully structured narrative. It is among the most artfully composed prose in the Hebrew Bible. The Hebrew text is approximately 1,300 words.
RUT.summary: Ruth narrates how a Moabite widow's loyalty to her Israelite mother-in-law leads to her incorporation into the Israelite community and into the ancestral line of King David.

SNG.author: The opening attribution — "The Song of Songs, which is Solomon's" (1:1) — uses the Hebrew asher li-Shelomoh, which can mean "by Solomon," "for Solomon," "about Solomon," or "in the style of Solomon." The language contains Aramaic features, a possible Persian loanword (pardes, "garden/orchard," 4:13), and a possible Greek loanword (appiryon, "sedan chair/palanquin," 3:9, possibly from Greek phoreion), suggesting a post-exilic date for the final form.
SNG.date: The linguistic evidence points to the 5th–3rd century BCE for composition or final editing, though the imagery may draw on older love poetry traditions.
SNG.genre: Lyric love poetry — a collection of poems exchanged between two lovers (a man and a woman) with occasional chorus ("daughters of Jerusalem"). Contains descriptive body-poems (Arabic: wasf), dialogue, monologue, and dream sequences.
SNG.summary: The Song of Solomon is a collection of erotic love poems celebrating physical desire, beauty, longing, and union between two lovers.

ZEC.author: Zechariah son of Berechiah son of Iddo (1:1), a priest-prophet contemporary with Haggai. However, chapters 9–14 differ so substantially in style, vocabulary, historical setting, and theology that most scholars identify them as a separate composition — "Deutero-Zechariah," dated later (possibly 5th–3rd century BCE). Chapters 9 and 12 each begin with the heading massa ("oracle"), which may mark separate collections.
ZEC.date: Chapters 1–8: 520–518 BCE (precisely dated, like Haggai). Chapters 9–14: uncertain, possibly 5th–3rd century BCE.
ZEC.genre: Prophetic vision (chapters 1–6 contain eight night visions — the most elaborate visionary sequence in the prophets), oracle, and proto-apocalyptic literature (chapters 9–14).
ZEC.summary: Zechariah moves from eight symbolic night visions encouraging the post-exilic community to a series of increasingly apocalyptic oracles about future judgment and restoration — the most heavily quoted Minor Prophet in the New Testament Passion narratives.

ZEP.author: Zephaniah son of Cushi son of Gedaliah son of Amariah son of Hezekiah (1:1) — the longest genealogy of any prophet, traced back four generations. If "Hezekiah" is King Hezekiah, Zephaniah is of royal descent. The name "Cushi" means "Cushite/Ethiopian," raising the possibility of African ancestry.
ZEP.date: The superscription places him during the reign of Josiah (640–609 BCE), likely before Josiah's reform (622 BCE), since the book condemns practices that the reform addressed.
ZEP.genre: Prophetic oracle — Day of YHWH poetry, oracles against nations, and restoration promises.
ZEP.summary: Zephaniah delivers the most intense Day of YHWH poetry in the prophets — cosmic judgment sweeping over Judah and all nations — followed by the promise of a purified remnant.

1CO.author: Paul (1:1), with Sosthenes. Universally accepted as authentically Pauline.
1CO.date: c. 53–55 CE, written from Ephesus (16:8). One of the earliest NT documents.
1CO.genre: Letter responding to specific questions and problems reported to Paul.
1CO.summary: 1 Corinthians addresses a fractured church dealing with divisions, sexual ethics, lawsuits, food sacrificed to idols, worship practices, spiritual gifts, and resurrection — providing the most detailed picture of a 1st-century Christian community's internal life.

1JN.author: Anonymous — unlike 2 and 3 John, 1 John has no sender identification. Traditionally attributed to the apostle John, the same author as the Gospel of John. The vocabulary, style, and theology overlap significantly with the Fourth Gospel (light/darkness, truth, love, abiding, world), but differences exist. Most scholars identify the author as someone within the "Johannine community" — possibly the same person as the Gospel's final editor or a close associate.
1JN.date: c. 90–110 CE, likely after the Gospel of John.
1JN.genre: Not a letter (no greeting, no sender, no addressee, no closing) — more accurately a homily, circular tract, or theological meditation.
1JN.summary: 1 John addresses a community torn by schism over the nature of Christ — insisting that true faith confesses Jesus came "in the flesh," true love requires action toward fellow believers, and the two are inseparable.

1PE.author: "Peter, an apostle of Jesus Christ" (1:1). The polished Greek style, use of the Septuagint, and the reference to "Babylon" (5:13, widely understood as a code name for Rome — used also in Revelation 14:8, 17:5, and Jewish apocalyptic texts like 4 Ezra and 2 Baruch, written after 70 CE) have led many scholars to question direct Petrine authorship. Silvanus (5:12 — "Through Silvanus... I have written") may have been a secretary or co-author. Others maintain Petrine authorship with secretarial assistance.
1PE.date: If Petrine: before c. 64–68 CE (Peter's traditional martyrdom under Nero). If pseudonymous: c. 70–90 CE.
1PE.genre: Letter — circular letter to multiple churches, combining encouragement under suffering with ethical instruction.
1PE.summary: 1 Peter addresses Christians as "resident aliens" suffering social hostility — encouraging them to maintain exemplary conduct in a hostile society while grounding their identity in the language of Israel's election and exile.

1TH.author: Paul (1:1), with Silvanus and Timothy. Universally accepted as authentically Pauline.
1TH.date: c. 49–51 CE — the earliest surviving Pauline letter and likely the earliest document in the New Testament.
1TH.genre: Letter — pastoral, warm, addressing community concerns about the fate of believers who have died before Christ's return.
1TH.summary: 1 Thessalonians is likely the oldest surviving Christian document — a letter of encouragement to a new church anxious about the delay of Christ's return and the fate of their dead.

1TI.author: Claims to be from Paul (1:1). 1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, and Titus form the "Pastoral Epistles" — a group that has been considered pseudonymous by the majority of critical scholars since the 19th century (F.C. Baur, H.J. Holtzmann). The evidence: vocabulary (306 words not found elsewhere in Paul; over one-third of the vocabulary is non-Pauline), style (longer, smoother sentences unlike Paul's argumentative prose), church organization (bishops, deacons, elders — a more developed institutional structure than in the undisputed letters), theology (emphasis on "sound teaching," "godliness," "faith" as doctrine rather than trust), and the inability to fit the biographical details into Acts' chronology. Defenders of authenticity argue Paul's vocabulary shifted over time and after release from a Roman imprisonment not recorded in Acts.
1TI.date: If Pauline: c. 62–65 CE. If pseudonymous: c. 80–100 CE.
1TI.genre: Letter of instruction for a community leader — a "church order" document.
1TI.summary: 1 Timothy provides instructions for organizing church life — addressing false teaching, qualifications for leaders, the behavior of different groups, and the management of a Christian community — in a document whose authorship is the most debated question in Pauline studies.

2CO.author: Paul (1:1), with Timothy. Authentically Pauline, though the letter's unity is debated.
2CO.genre: Letter — the most personal and emotionally intense of Paul's letters. Possibly a composite of multiple letters.
2CO.summary: 2 Corinthians is Paul's most personal letter — defending his apostleship against rival "super-apostles," revealing his suffering and weakness, and containing the most autobiographical material in the Pauline corpus.

2JN.author: "The elder" (ho presbyteros, v. 1) — not named. Traditionally identified with the apostle John. Papias (in Eusebius, Church History 3.39.4) distinguishes between "John the apostle" and "John the elder" — whether these are the same person or two different figures is debated.
2JN.genre: Letter — the shortest book in the NT by word count (in Greek). A genuine letter with greeting, body, and closing.
2JN.summary: 2 John is a brief letter from "the elder" warning a church against receiving traveling teachers who deny that Jesus Christ came in the flesh — the same docetic threat addressed in 1 John.

2PE.author: Claims to be "Simon Peter, a servant and apostle of Jesus Christ" (1:1) and references the transfiguration as an eyewitness (1:16–18). However, 2 Peter is the most widely questioned book in the NT regarding authorship. The evidence: it was the last NT book accepted into the canon, it was unknown to many early church writers, its Greek style differs markedly from 1 Peter, it borrows extensively from Jude (chapter 2 parallels Jude 4–18 almost verbatim), and it refers to Paul's letters as a collection that some distort "as they do the other Scriptures" (3:15–16) — implying Paul's letters are already "Scripture," suggesting a later date.
2PE.date: c. 100–130 CE — likely the latest-written book in the New Testament.
2PE.genre: Testament/farewell letter (1:13–15 — Peter knows his death is approaching) combined with anti-heretical polemic.
2PE.summary: 2 Peter is likely the last-written NT document — a defense against false teachers and against doubts about Christ's return, asserting that the delay is patience, not abandonment.

2TH.author: Claims to be from Paul (1:1, 3:17). Authenticity is debated — the eschatology appears to differ from 1 Thessalonians (imminent return vs. events that must happen first), the style closely mimics 1 Thessalonians' structure (unusually close for a genuine second letter), and the tone is more formal. Scholars are divided.
2TH.date: If Pauline: c. 50–52 CE (shortly after 1 Thessalonians). If pseudonymous: c. 80–100 CE.
2TH.genre: Letter addressing eschatological confusion and community discipline.
2TH.summary: 2 Thessalonians addresses a community in eschatological panic — clarifying that the Day of the Lord has not yet come and will be preceded by specific events, including the revelation of "the man of lawlessness."

2TI.author: Claims to be from Paul (1:1). Part of the Pastoral Epistles — the authorship debate applies here as with 1 Timothy. However, 2 Timothy is the most personal of the three Pastorals, and some scholars who consider 1 Timothy and Titus pseudonymous argue 2 Timothy may contain genuine Pauline fragments (especially 4:6–22).
2TI.date: If Pauline: c. 64–67 CE (Paul's final imprisonment, before execution). If pseudonymous: c. 80–100 CE.
2TI.genre: Testament/farewell letter — a literary genre well attested in Jewish and Greco-Roman literature (Testament of Moses, Testaments of the Twelve Patriarchs, Socrates' farewell in Plato's Phaedo).
2TI.summary: 2 Timothy reads as Paul's last testament — a farewell from prison urging Timothy to guard the faith, endure suffering, and continue the work, written with an awareness of approaching death.

3JN.author: "The elder" (ho presbyteros, v. 1) — the same designation as 2 John.
3JN.genre: Personal letter — the most genuinely personal letter in the NT after Philemon.
3JN.summary: 3 John is a personal letter praising Gaius for his hospitality to traveling missionaries, condemning Diotrephes for refusing the elder's authority, and commending Demetrius — a window into the politics of early church leadership.

ACT.author: Anonymous; the same author as Luke's Gospel (both address "Theophilus," 1:1; Luke 1:3). Traditionally identified as Luke, Paul's companion.
ACT.date: c. 80–90 CE (after Luke's Gospel, which it presupposes). Some scholars argue for an earlier date (before 64 CE) based on the abrupt ending, which does not narrate Paul's death.
ACT.genre: Ancient historiography — specifically, a narrative of the early church's expansion. The closest genre parallels are Hellenistic histories (Polybius, Josephus) and ancient association narratives.
ACT.summary: Acts narrates the expansion of the early church from Jerusalem to Rome — from a Jewish sect in Palestine to a movement encompassing the Gentile world — structured around the geographic spread outlined in 1:8: "Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, and to the ends of the earth."

COL.author: The letter claims to be from Paul (1:1), with Timothy. Colossians is among the disputed Pauline letters — some scholars accept Pauline authorship; others consider it pseudonymous based on vocabulary, style, and theological development (cosmic Christology, realized eschatology differing from earlier Paul).
COL.date: If Pauline: c. 60–62 CE (during imprisonment). If pseudonymous: c. 70–80 CE.
COL.genre: Letter addressing a specific "philosophy" threatening the community.
COL.summary: Colossians contains a cosmic christological hymn declaring Christ as the image of the invisible God and the firstborn of all creation — written to counter a "philosophy" that combined Jewish and pagan elements threatening to diminish Christ's supremacy.

EPH.author: The letter claims to be from Paul (1:1, 3:1). However, Ephesians is among the "disputed" Pauline letters — a significant number of scholars consider it pseudonymous (written by a follower of Paul), based on vocabulary (over 40 words not found elsewhere in Paul), style (long, complex sentences — 1:3–14 is one sentence in Greek), theology (cosmic ecclesiology, realized eschatology), and literary relationship to Colossians (extensive verbal parallels).
EPH.date: If Pauline: c. 60–62 CE (during imprisonment). If pseudonymous: c. 80–90 CE.
EPH.genre: Letter — though its lack of personal greetings, specific situations, and local references makes it more like a theological treatise in letter form.
EPH.summary: Ephesians presents the most developed ecclesiology in the New Testament — the church as the body of Christ, the mystery of Jew-Gentile unity, and the cosmic scope of God's plan to "unite all things in Christ."

GAL.author: Paul (1:1). Universally accepted as authentically Pauline.
GAL.date: c. 48–55 CE — one of Paul's earliest letters. The date depends on whether "Galatia" refers to the ethnic region in central Anatolia (North Galatian hypothesis) or the Roman province including southern cities like Pisidian Antioch, Iconium, Lystra, and Derbe (South Galatian hypothesis).
GAL.genre: Letter — polemical, urgent, written in Paul's most heated tone.
GAL.summary: Galatians is Paul's most passionate letter — a fierce defense of his gospel of justification by faith apart from works of the Torah, written in anger against opponents who were requiring Gentile converts to be circumcised.

HEB.author: Unknown. The text is anonymous — the title "To the Hebrews" was added later. Paul was traditionally proposed, but Origen (3rd century CE) famously concluded: "Who wrote the epistle, in truth God knows" (preserved in Eusebius, Church History 6.25.14). The Greek style is the most polished in the NT (arguably superior even to Luke), the vocabulary is distinctive, and the theological framework differs from Paul's. Apollos (suggested by Luther), Barnabas (suggested by Tertullian), Priscilla, and others have been proposed. The author cannot be identified.
HEB.date: Before the destruction of the temple (70 CE) if the present-tense references to sacrificial worship (8:4–5, 9:6–9, 10:1–3) reflect an active temple; or c. 60–90 CE if these references are theoretical/literary. The mention of Timothy (13:23) suggests a Pauline circle connection.
HEB.genre: Not a letter — it lacks an opening greeting. The author calls it a "word of exhortation" (logos tēs paraklēseōs, 13:22) — the same phrase used in Acts 13:15 for a synagogue sermon. It is an extended homily or theological treatise with a letter-like closing (13:18–25).
HEB.summary: Hebrews argues that Christ is the superior mediator of a new and better covenant — surpassing angels, Moses, Joshua, the Levitical priesthood, and the tabernacle sacrifices — using the most sustained scriptural argument in the New Testament.

JAS.author: "James, a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ" (1:1). Traditionally identified as James the brother of Jesus (Mark 6:3, Galatians 1:19), the leader of the Jerusalem church (Acts 15:13, 21:18). The polished Greek style has led some scholars to question whether a Galilean artisan could have produced it, though Greek literacy was widespread in 1st-century Palestine. Others propose a pseudonymous author writing in James's name. The debate is unresolved.
JAS.date: If by James: before 62 CE (Josephus records James's execution in 62 CE, Antiquities 20.200). If pseudonymous: c. 70–100 CE.
JAS.genre: Paraenesis — moral exhortation. James resembles Jewish wisdom literature (Proverbs, Sirach) more than a typical letter. It moves rapidly from topic to topic with minimal sustained argument.
JAS.summary: James is a practical letter of ethical exhortation — emphasizing action over mere profession, care for the poor, control of the tongue, and the inseparability of faith and works — the most "Jewish" document in the New Testament.

JHN.author: Anonymous. The text refers to "the disciple whom Jesus loved" as the source of testimony (21:24), but does not name him. Early tradition (Irenaeus, c. 180 CE) identifies this figure as John son of Zebedee. Many scholars distinguish between the Beloved Disciple (a historical figure behind the tradition), the evangelist (who composed the Gospel), and a later editor (who added chapter 21 and possibly other material). The "Johannine community" hypothesis proposes a distinct community that produced the Gospel, 1–3 John, and possibly Revelation.
JHN.date: c. 90–100 CE, though some scholars argue for earlier dates. The relationship to the Synoptics is debated — John may know Mark or share common oral tradition but follows a different narrative framework.
JHN.genre: Ancient biography (bios), but radically different in structure, style, and theology from the Synoptics.
JHN.summary: John is the most theologically developed Gospel — opening with a cosmic prologue identifying Jesus as the pre-existent Word (Logos), structured around seven signs and seven "I am" statements, and presenting a Jesus who speaks in extended discourses rather than parables.

JUD.author: "Jude, a servant of Jesus Christ and a brother of James" (v. 1). If James is the brother of Jesus, Jude is also Jesus' brother (Mark 6:3 lists "James and Joses and Judas and Simon" as Jesus' brothers). Whether the letter is by this Jude or a later author writing in his name is debated.
JUD.date: c. 65–90 CE. The letter warns against false teachers who have "secretly slipped in" (v. 4), suggesting an established community facing internal disruption.
JUD.genre: Letter — brief, polemical, dense with allusion.
JUD.summary: Jude is a short, fiery letter warning against false teachers — notable for quoting two non-canonical Jewish texts (1 Enoch and the Assumption of Moses) as authoritative.

LUK.author: Anonymous. The text's prologue (1:1–4) identifies the author as a researcher who investigated eyewitness testimony and wrote "an orderly account" for "Theophilus." Early tradition (Irenaeus, c. 180 CE; the Muratorian Fragment, c. 170–200 CE) identifies the author as Luke, Paul's companion (Colossians 4:14, Philemon 24, 2 Timothy 4:11). The "we" passages in Acts (16:10–17, 20:5–15, 21:1–18, 27:1–28:16) are cited as evidence, though their interpretation is debated.
LUK.date: c. 80–90 CE. Luke uses Mark (c. 65–70 CE) and reflects knowledge of Jerusalem's destruction (19:43–44, 21:20–24 are more specific than Mark's parallels).
LUK.genre: Ancient biography (bios), presented as a historiographical project (1:1–4). Luke-Acts is a two-volume work — the longest contribution by a single author in the New Testament.
LUK.summary: Luke is the most literary of the Gospels — a carefully researched narrative emphasizing Jesus' concern for the poor, outcasts, women, and Gentiles, written as the first volume of a two-part work that continues in Acts.

MAT.author: The text is anonymous — no internal attribution. The title "According to Matthew" (kata Matthaion) was added by the 2nd century CE. Papias (c. 130 CE, preserved in Eusebius, Church History 3.39.16) states that "Matthew collected the oracles (logia) in the Hebrew language, and each interpreted them as best he could." Whether this refers to the canonical Gospel — which was composed in Greek, not translated from Hebrew — is debated. The author demonstrates detailed knowledge of Jewish law, scripture, and interpretive methods.
MAT.date: c. 80–90 CE is the majority scholarly estimate. The destruction of the temple (70 CE) appears to be reflected in 22:7 ("The king was enraged. He sent his army and destroyed those murderers and burned their city"). Matthew uses Mark as a source, placing it after Mark (c. 65–70 CE).
MAT.genre: Ancient biography (bios) — a narrative of Jesus' life organized thematically and theologically, not strictly chronologically.
MAT.summary: Matthew presents Jesus as the Jewish Messiah who fulfills the Hebrew scriptures — structuring his teaching into five major discourses, opening with a genealogy tracing David's line, and quoting the Old Testament more than any other Gospel.

MRK.author: Anonymous. The title "According to Mark" was added later. Papias (c. 130 CE, in Eusebius, Church History 3.39.15) identifies the author as "Mark," Peter's interpreter, who wrote down Peter's teachings "not in order." Early tradition identifies this Mark with John Mark of Acts 12:12, 25.
MRK.date: c. 65–70 CE — the earliest canonical Gospel. The "little apocalypse" (chapter 13) may reflect the Jewish War (66–70 CE) and the anticipation of the temple's destruction.
MRK.genre: Ancient biography (bios) — though Mark invented something new: a narrative of Jesus' life centered on his death.
MRK.summary: Mark is the earliest and shortest Gospel — a fast-paced narrative that emphasizes Jesus' actions over his teaching, develops the "messianic secret," and originally ended abruptly at an empty tomb with women fleeing in fear.

PHM.author: Paul (v. 1), with Timothy. Universally accepted as authentically Pauline.
PHM.date: c. 55–62 CE, written from prison (v. 1, 9–10, 23). The same imprisonment as Philippians and Colossians (if those are Pauline).
PHM.genre: Personal letter — the shortest and most private of Paul's letters. Also a masterpiece of rhetorical persuasion.
PHM.summary: Philemon is a personal letter from Paul to a slave owner, asking him to receive back his runaway slave Onesimus — now a Christian — "no longer as a slave, but better than a slave, as a dear brother."

PHP.author: Paul (1:1), with Timothy. Universally accepted as authentically Pauline.
PHP.date: c. 55–62 CE — written from prison, but which imprisonment is debated: Rome (c. 60–62 CE, traditional view), Ephesus (c. 54–55 CE, no direct evidence but inferred from 1 Corinthians 15:32, 2 Corinthians 1:8–10), or Caesarea (c. 58–60 CE).
PHP.genre: Letter — warm, personal, joyful despite imprisonment. Possibly a composite of multiple letters (the abrupt shift at 3:2).
PHP.summary: Philippians is Paul's warmest letter — a joyful communication from prison containing the great christological hymn (2:6–11) and Paul's most personal reflections on suffering, contentment, and the surpassing worth of knowing Christ.

REV.author: "John" (1:1, 4, 9; 22:8), who identifies himself as a servant and prophet, not as an apostle. He is on the island of Patmos "because of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus" (1:9). Whether this is the apostle John, John the elder (mentioned by Papias), or another John is debated. The Greek of Revelation is markedly different from the Gospel of John — rougher, with grammatical irregularities that some scholars attribute to a native Aramaic/Hebrew speaker writing in Greek (a "Semitic Greek" style). Dionysius of Alexandria (3rd century, in Eusebius, Church History 7.25) already argued on linguistic grounds that Revelation and the Gospel could not share the same author.
REV.date: c. 90–96 CE, during the reign of Domitian (81–96 CE) — the traditional and majority date. Some scholars argue for a Neronian date (c. 65–68 CE) based on internal evidence (the seven kings of 17:9–11).
REV.genre: Apocalyptic literature — the genre's defining example (apokalypsis, "unveiling/revelation," gives the genre its name). Also prophecy (1:3, 22:7, 10, 18–19) and letter (1:4–5, epistolary greeting; 22:21, letter closing).
REV.summary: Revelation is the New Testament's only full apocalypse — a visionary text of extraordinary symbolic density, drawing on Daniel, Ezekiel, Isaiah, and Zechariah to depict cosmic conflict between God and evil, culminating in the destruction of "Babylon," the last judgment, and the descent of a new Jerusalem.

ROM.author: Paul (1:1). Romans is universally accepted as authentically Pauline. Tertius, the scribe, identifies himself in 16:22 — "I, Tertius, who wrote down this letter, greet you in the Lord."
ROM.date: c. 55–57 CE, written from Corinth during Paul's third missionary journey (Acts 20:2–3). Paul mentions his plan to deliver the Jerusalem collection and then visit Rome en route to Spain (15:22–29).
ROM.genre: Letter (epistle) — the longest and most systematic of Paul's letters.
ROM.summary: Romans is Paul's most comprehensive letter — a sustained argument about how the God of Israel has acted through Jesus the Messiah to bring both Jews and Gentiles into one covenant people, addressing the relationship between faith, law, and righteousness.

TIT.author: Claims to be from Paul (1:1). Part of the Pastoral Epistles — the same authorship debate applies.
TIT.date: If Pauline: c. 62–65 CE. If pseudonymous: c. 80–100 CE.
TIT.genre: Letter of instruction for a community organizer.
TIT.summary: Titus is a compact letter about establishing church order on Crete — appointing elders, countering false teachers, and instructing different social groups in conduct consistent with "sound doctrine."
